perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it will not export, re -export, resell or transfer, or otherwise ship or deliver any Proprietary Information, product, assembly, component or any technical data or software which violates any export control s or limitations imposed by the United States or any other governmental authority, or to any country for which an export license or other governmental approval is required at the time of export without first obtaining all necessary licenses or other approv als.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it will not export, re -export, resell or transfer, or otherwise ship or deliver any Proprietary Information, product, assembly, component or any technical data or software.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause about export controls and the need for licenses, the contract now contradicts export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation requires compliance with export control laws. By omitting the requirement to obtain necessary licenses, the modified contract conflicts with this regulation.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes a clause that explicitly prohibits the export, re-export, or transfer of proprietary information in violation of U.S. export control laws. It also requires obtaining necessary licenses before exporting when required. The changed text removes this entire clause. The Perturbation Explanation states that this omission creates a contradiction with export control regulations. The Law Explanation states that the modified contract conflicts with regulations requiring compliance with export control laws by omitting the need to obtain necessary licenses. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Title 15 of the eCFR, which deals with commerce and foreign trade regulations, and suggests compliance with export control laws is necessary. While Snippet 2 is a 'Document Not Found' error, the remaining evidence sufficiently demonstrates that the omission of the export control clause directly contradicts export control laws, thus resulting in a clear legal contradiction, as the contract no longer mandates compliance with these laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania without regard to principles of conflicts of law. The parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the court s located in the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania as appropriate, in litigating any dispute hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania without regard to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause agreeing to submit to the jurisdiction of Pennsylvania courts could contradict laws related to jurisdiction and venue, especially if the company is not located in Pennsylvania.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law addresses proper venue for civil actions. Removing the clause about jurisdiction could lead to disputes over where a lawsuit should be filed, potentially violating the spirit and intent of ensuring a fair and convenient venue.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause regarding submission to the jurisdiction of Pennsylvania courts directly contradicts the principles outlined in 28 U.S. Code § 1391, which governs venue in civil actions. The original text ensured clarity regarding where disputes would be litigated. By removing this clause, the modified text creates uncertainty and potential disputes over venue, undermining the purpose of establishing a clear and convenient forum as contemplated by venue statutes.

perturbation.original_text: NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES REGARDING ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION AND THE PROPRIETARY INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”. EACH PARTY DISCLAIMS ALL EXPRESS OR IMPLIED WARRANTIES WITH RESPECT TO ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ALL IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND THE WARRANTY OF NON -INFRINGEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES REGARDING ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION AND THE PROPRIETARY INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit disclaimer of warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, and non-infringement, the contract creates ambiguity and may unintentionally imply certain warranties where they should be disclaimed, especially under Pennsylvania's interpretation of the UCC.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 13 Pa.C.S. § 2316 - Exclusion or modification of warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania's adoption of the UCC requires specific language for disclaiming warranties, especially regarding merchantability and fitness. Omitting these specific disclaimers increases the risk of implied warranty claims, contradicting the intent of providing information 'as is'.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly disclaims warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, and non-infringement. The changed text removes this explicit disclaimer. According to the Perturbation and Law Explanations, under Pennsylvania's interpretation of the UCC, omitting these specific disclaimers increases the risk of implied warranty claims, which contradicts the original intent of providing information 'as is'. Since the original text contained a full disclaimer, and the changed text omits parts of this disclaimer, the legal protection granted by the disclaimer is weakened, exposing the involved party to more potential risk.

perturbation.original_text: Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that Prop rietary Information may be disclosed under this Agreement with appropriate restrictive legends provided thereon to the U.S. Government under the provisions of FAR, or similar regulations of other Government agencies limiting use and disclosure, but only to the extent such limited use or disclosure is specifically related to the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that Prop rietary Information may be disclosed under this Agreement to the U.S. Government.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for 'appropriate restrictive legends' and the clause about FAR and similar regulations contradicts federal regulations concerning the protection of proprietary information when disclosed to the government.
perturbation.law_explanation: FAR 52.227-14 outlines the rights in data, including proprietary information, provided to the government. Removing the restrictive legends and limitations clause undermines the protection afforded to the disclosing party's proprietary information under this regulation.

As prescribed in 27.409 (b)(1) , insert the following clause with any appropriate alternates: Rights in Data-General (May 2014) (a) Definitions. As used in this clause- Computer database or "database means" a collection of recorded information in a form capable of, and for the purpose of, being stored in, processed, and operated on by a computer. The term does not include computer software. Computer software - (1) Means (i) Computer programs that comprise a series of instructions, rules, routines, or statements, regardless of the media in which recorded, that allow or cause a computer to perform a specific operation or series of operations; and (ii) Recorded information comprising source code listings, design details, algorithms, processes, flow charts, formulas, and related material that would enable the computer program to be produced, created, or compiled. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 48 CFR § 52.227-14 - General. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 48 CFR § 52.227-14 - General. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next 52.227-14 Rights in Data—General. As prescribed in 27.409(b)(1) , insert the following clause with any appropriate alternates: Rights in Data—General (MAY 2014) (a) Definitions. As used in this clause— Computer database or database means a collection of recorded information in a form capable of, and for the purpose of, being stored in, processed, and operated on by a computer. The term does not include computer software . Computer software —(1) Means (i) Computer programs that comprise a series of instructions, rules, routines, or statements, regardless of the media in which recorded, that allow or cause a computer to perform a specific operation or series of operations; and (ii) Recorded information comprising source code listings, design details, algorithms, processes, flow charts, formulas, and related material that would enable the computer program to be produced, created, or compiled. (2) Does not include computer databases or computer software documentation . Computer software documentation means owner's manuals, user's manuals, installation instructions, operating instructions, and other similar items, regardless of storage medium, that explain the capabilities of the computer software or provide instructions for using the software. Data means recorded information , regardless of form or the media on which it may be recorded. The term includes technical data and computer software . The term does not include information incidental to contract administration, such as financial, administrative, cost or pricing , or management information . Form, fit, and function data means data relating to items, components, or processes that are sufficient to enable physical and functional interchangeability, and data identifying source , size, configurat
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for 'appropriate restrictive legends' on proprietary information disclosed to the U.S. Government, and removes the clause referencing FAR regulations. FAR 52.227-14, as indicated in both scraped snippets, concerns 'Rights in Data-General' and implies procedures for protecting proprietary information. By omitting the restrictive legends and the clause referencing FAR, the modified text contradicts the original contract's commitment to adhering to regulations that protect proprietary data disclosed to the government, creating a clear contradiction.
